FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study wave 1 release, n=6758 from US adolescents

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured; abstract states 'more screen time' as comparative finding across neurodevelopmental profiles (B5 qualifier: screen time is qualifying variable; applies B1)

FT_IC3.evidence: Latent profile analysis with inferential comparisons; chi-square tests and F-tests comparing profiles on screen time (χ²=6.80, p<.0001)

FT_IC4.evidence: F=6.80, p<.0001 reported in abstract for screen time differences across profiles

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; latent profile analysis with clinical/behavioral outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is not the dependent variable; it is one of many clinical correlates of neurodevelopmental profiles (profiles are DV). Multiple non-demographic predictors examined (neural features, cognitive performance, psychiatric diagnoses). Does not meet EC7 criterion (a).
notes: Paper examines screen time as a clinical correlate/outcome correlate of brain-based developmental profiles rather than SM/smartphone as primary outcome. Screen time is one of several variables associated with different neurodevelopmental subgroups.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, n=5412 US adolescents aged 10-14, May 2020 COVID-19 survey

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time modalities: texting, social media, video chatting, multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, streaming videos/movies/TV (B1: separate coefficients reported for distinct SM and phone activities)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression; Table 2 reports adjusted regression coefficients (B) with 95% CI and p-values for associations between mental health/resiliency factors and total screen use

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2: B coefficients with 95% CI reported (e.g., mental health B=0.29, 95% CI 0.06-0.52, p=.01; perceived stress B=0.67, 95% CI 0.43-0.91, p<.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with SM→outcome relationships examined

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in JAMA Pediatrics, no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (IV), not dependent variable. Paper examines SM/screen time → mental health and resiliency outcomes (inverse direction to EC7). Does not meet EC7 criterion (a).
notes: Cross-sectional COVID-19 pandemic study examining screen use disparities and mental health correlates. Separate coefficients reported for multiple screen modalities as required by B1.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, n=5335 US adolescents aged 10-14, May 2020 pandemic data

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time modalities examined: texting, social media, video chatting, multiplayer gaming (B1: separate coefficient comparisons across screen categories reported in Tables 2 and 4)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple statistical tests: paired t-tests comparing parent vs. adolescent reports (Table 2: t=10.58, p<.001 for total; t=10.89, p<.001 for texting); linear regression analyses (Table 4) examining sociodemographic predictors of parent-adolescent discrepancies

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2: t-statistics with p-values and ICC reported. Table 4: B coefficients with 95% CI (e.g., Age B=0.20, 95% CI 0.11-0.29, p<.001; Latino/Hispanic B=0.30, 95% CI 0.02-0.58, p=.04)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD parent-adolescent data examining screen time measurement concordance and sociodemographic predictors of discrepancies

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Academic Pediatrics, no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable being compared (parent-reported vs. adolescent-reported); paper examines sociodemographic predictors of discrepancies in screen time reports, not SM→outcome relationships. Non-demographic predictors (reporter type/measurement factors) examined. Does not meet EC7 criterion (a).
notes: Parent-adolescent concordance study examining recreational screen time reporting discrepancies across multiple categories with sociodemographic correlates. Separate coefficients reported for distinct screen modalities per B1.
